questions.question: Why can retries make an outage worse instead of better?
options: They multiply load on already stressed dependencies and can amplify queueing and tail latency.
They automatically disable deadline propagation at every service hop.
They guarantee duplicate writes even for read-only operations.
They remove the need for admission control in overloaded systems.
questions.explanation: Retries are extra work, so they can worsen overload when a dependency is already struggling.

questions.question: What is the main job of a timeout in a distributed system?
options: To make every request fail at the same latency so dashboards stay tidy.
To bound how long work is allowed to consume resources before the caller gives up.
To guarantee that any failed request is safe to retry automatically.
To replace observability with a simpler failure signal.
questions.explanation: Timeouts are resource and latency bounds, not universal retry permission.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to propagate deadlines across service hops?
options: It lets each dependency see how much latency budget remains before doing more work.
It makes all retries globally coordinated without additional logic.
It guarantees no request will ever need a fallback response.
It removes the need to classify retryable errors.
questions.explanation: Propagated deadlines help each hop make budget-aware decisions instead of guessing.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals retry amplification during an incident?
options: Attempts per original request rising sharply alongside dependency saturation.
The number of source files in the repository increasing over time.
Median latency staying unchanged during a severe outage.
The total number of developers who touched the service that quarter.
questions.explanation: Attempt-per-request ratio exposes how much extra work retries are creating.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of adding jitter to backoff?
options: You reduce synchronized retry waves, but individual retries become less predictable to reason about by wall-clock time.
You make retries free from a capacity perspective.
You ensure every request succeeds on the second attempt.
You eliminate the need for idempotency.
questions.explanation: Jitter improves fleet behavior by spreading retries out, even though it makes exact timing less regular.

questions.question: Which failure should usually not be retried blindly?
options: A non-idempotent operation that may already have committed before the timeout was observed.
A brief network reset on an idempotent read.
A transient connection failure during a health probe.
A rare TLS handshake timeout on a safe GET request.
questions.explanation: If the side effect may already have happened, a blind retry can create correctness bugs.

questions.question: Why is it dangerous for every layer in a call chain to have its own retry loop?
options: Nested retries can multiply total attempts across the fanout path and create a storm.
Each layer will automatically share the same jitter schedule and cancel each other.
The root caller no longer needs to set a deadline.
It forces every dependency to behave as strongly consistent storage.
questions.explanation: Independent retry loops across layers can explode total work during a failure.

questions.question: What is a strong senior-level answer to 'How many retries should we do?'
options: It depends on failure mode, remaining deadline budget, idempotency, and how much extra load the system can tolerate.
Always retry exactly three times because that is the standard distributed-systems rule.
Retry until the dependency responds because availability matters more than overload.
Never retry anything because timeouts already provide enough reliability.
questions.explanation: Retry count is a workload and capacity decision, not a universal constant.
